title: Phishing Awareness 101
description: Learn to identify phishing emails before they compromise your organization.
content:
# Phishing Awareness 101

Phishing is the #1 attack vector in cybersecurity.

## Key Warning Signs
- Urgency or threats
- Mismatched sender domains
- Generic greetings
- Suspicious links
- Unexpected attachments

## The Hover Test
Always hover over links to see the real URL before clicking.

## What To Do
1. Don't click any links
2. Report to IT Security
3. Delete the email

title: Password Hygiene & Management
description: Best practices for creating and managing strong, unique passwords.
content:
# Password Hygiene & Management

Weak passwords are the second most common cause of data breaches.

## Strong Password Rules
- Minimum 12 characters
- Mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols
- Never reuse passwords
- Use a passphrase

## Password Managers
Use a password manager to generate and store unique passwords.

## Multi-Factor Authentication
Always enable MFA on email, banking, and work accounts.

title: Social Engineering Defense
description: Recognize and resist psychological manipulation tactics used by attackers.
content:
# Social Engineering Defense

Social engineering exploits human psychology, not technology.

## Common Tactics
- Authority: Impersonating executives or IT
- Urgency: Act now or lose access
- Scarcity: Only 1 hour left

## Defense Strategies
1. Verify identity through a separate channel
2. Never give credentials over phone or email
3. It is OK to say I need to verify this first
4. Report suspicious requests to your manager

title: HIPAA Security Awareness
description: Understand your obligations under HIPAA to protect patient health information.
content:
# HIPAA Security Awareness

HIPAA requires covered entities to protect Protected Health Information (PHI).

## What is PHI?
Any information that can identify a patient: name, DOB, SSN, medical record numbers, diagnosis, treatment.

## Your Obligations
- Only access PHI you need for your job
- Never share PHI via unencrypted email
- Lock your screen when away from your desk
- Report any suspected breach immediately

## Phishing and HIPAA
Phishing is the #1 cause of healthcare data breaches. A single click can expose thousands of patient records.

title: PCI DSS Cardholder Data Protection
description: Protect payment card data and meet PCI DSS v4.0 requirements.
content:
# PCI DSS Cardholder Data Protection

PCI DSS v4.0 Requirement 12.6.3 mandates phishing simulation training.

## What is Cardholder Data?
- Primary Account Number (PAN)
- Cardholder name, expiration date
- CVV/CVC codes (never store these)

## Your Responsibilities
- Never write down or photograph card numbers
- Never send card data via email or chat
- Use only approved payment systems
- Report any suspected card data exposure immediately

title: GDPR Data Privacy Fundamentals
description: Understand GDPR requirements for handling personal data of EU residents.
content:
# GDPR Data Privacy Fundamentals

GDPR applies to any organization handling data of EU residents.

## Key Principles
- Lawful basis: Have a legal reason to process data
- Data minimization: Collect only what you need
- Purpose limitation: Use data only for stated purposes

## Individual Rights
EU residents have the right to access, correct, and delete their data.

## Breach Notification
GDPR requires breach notification within 72 hours.

title: Ransomware Prevention & Response
description: How ransomware works and how to prevent and respond to attacks.
content:
# Ransomware Prevention & Response

Ransomware cost organizations $20 billion globally in 2023.

## How Ransomware Spreads
1. Phishing email with malicious attachment
2. Compromised credentials
3. Unpatched software
4. Malicious USB drives

## Prevention
- Never open unexpected attachments
- Keep software updated
- Use MFA on all remote access
- Back up data regularly

## If You Suspect Infection
1. Disconnect from the network immediately
2. Do not pay the ransom
3. Call IT Security immediately

title: Safe Web Browsing
description: Identify malicious websites and practice safe browsing habits.
content:
# Safe Web Browsing

Malicious websites can install malware or steal credentials.

## URL Red Flags
- Misspellings: paypa1.com, arnazon.com
- Extra subdomains: login.microsoft.com.evil.com
- HTTP (not HTTPS) for login pages

## Safe Browsing Habits
- Type URLs directly instead of clicking links
- Check for the padlock icon (HTTPS)
- Do not download software from unofficial sites
- Keep your browser updated

title: Mobile Device Security
description: Secure your smartphone and tablet against common threats.
content:
# Mobile Device Security

Mobile devices are increasingly targeted because they carry sensitive data.

## Essential Security Steps
- Use a strong PIN or biometric lock
- Enable full-disk encryption
- Keep OS and apps updated
- Only install apps from official stores
- Enable remote wipe capability

## Mobile Phishing
SMS phishing (smishing) is growing rapidly. The small screen makes it harder to spot fake URLs.

title: Business Email Compromise (BEC)
description: Recognize and prevent costly BEC attacks including CEO fraud and wire transfer scams.
content:
# Business Email Compromise (BEC)

BEC caused $2.9 billion in losses in 2023.

## Common BEC Scenarios
- CEO Fraud: Attacker impersonates CEO requesting urgent wire transfer
- Vendor Impersonation: Fake invoice with changed bank details
- Payroll Diversion: Request to change direct deposit

## Red Flags
- Urgency and secrecy
- Request to bypass normal approval processes
- New bank account or wire instructions

## Defense
Always verify wire transfers via a phone call to a known number.

title: Insider Threat Awareness
description: Understand how insider threats occur and how to report concerns safely.
content:
# Insider Threat Awareness

Insider threats account for 34% of all data breaches.

## Types of Insider Threats
- Malicious: Employee intentionally stealing data
- Negligent: Employee accidentally exposing data
- Compromised: Employee credentials stolen by an attacker

## Warning Signs
- Accessing data outside normal job scope
- Downloading large amounts of data before resignation
- Bypassing security controls

## How to Report
Report concerns to your manager, HR, or anonymously through the ethics hotline.

title: Physical Security & Tailgating
description: Protect physical access to your workplace and prevent tailgating.
content:
# Physical Security & Tailgating

Physical security breaches can be just as damaging as cyber attacks.

## Tailgating
Never hold doors open for people you do not recognize. Direct them to reception.

## Clean Desk Policy
- Lock your computer when away from your desk
- Do not leave sensitive documents visible
- Shred documents before discarding
- Do not write passwords on sticky notes

## Visitor Management
- All visitors must sign in and wear a badge
- Escort visitors at all times

title: Secure Remote Work Practices
description: Stay secure while working from home or public locations.
content:
# Secure Remote Work Practices

Remote work has expanded the attack surface significantly.

## Home Network Security
- Use your company VPN at all times
- Change your router default password
- Keep router firmware updated
- Use WPA3 or WPA2 encryption

## Physical Security at Home
- Position your screen away from windows
- Use a privacy screen filter
- Lock your computer when stepping away

## Video Calls
- Be aware of what is visible in your background
- Verify meeting links before joining

title: Cloud Security Basics
description: Understand cloud security risks and best practices for using SaaS and cloud services.
content:
# Cloud Security Basics

Cloud services are convenient but introduce new security risks if misconfigured.

## Shared Responsibility Model
- Cloud provider: Secures the infrastructure
- You: Secure your data, access controls, and configurations

## Common Cloud Risks
- Misconfigured public storage buckets
- Weak or shared credentials
- Excessive permissions
- Shadow IT (unauthorized cloud services)

## Best Practices
- Use SSO and MFA for all cloud services
- Review who has access to shared files regularly
- Never store sensitive data in personal cloud accounts

title: Incident Reporting & Response
description: Know when and how to report a security incident.
content:
# Incident Reporting & Response

Fast reporting is critical. The average cost of a breach increases by 30% for every week it goes unreported.

## What to Report
- Clicked a phishing link
- Opened a suspicious attachment
- Lost or stolen device
- Noticed unusual account activity

## How to Report
1. Do not panic
2. Contact IT Security immediately
3. Preserve evidence
4. Document what happened and when

## What Happens Next
IT Security will investigate, contain the threat, and notify affected parties.

title: Vishing & Phone Scam Defense
description: Recognize voice phishing and phone-based social engineering attacks.
content:
# Vishing & Phone Scam Defense

Vishing uses phone calls to steal credentials or financial information.

## Common Vishing Scenarios
- IT Support Scam: This is Microsoft support, we detected a virus
- IRS Scam: You owe back taxes and will be arrested
- Bank Fraud: We detected fraud, please verify your card number
- Internal IT: This is the help desk, we need your password

## Defense Rules
- IT will NEVER ask for your password over the phone
- Hang up and call back on a verified number
- Report suspicious calls to IT Security

title: USB & Removable Media Security
description: Understand the dangers of unknown USB devices and removable media.
content:
# USB & Removable Media Security

A USB drive found in a parking lot is one of the oldest attack vectors.

## The Danger
Malicious USB drives can automatically install malware, emulate a keyboard, or exfiltrate data silently.

## The Rule
Never plug in a USB drive you did not purchase yourself from a trusted retailer. This includes drives found in parking lots or received as gifts at conferences.

## Authorized Media Only
Use only company-approved storage devices. Use approved cloud storage for file transfers.

title: CMMC & DFARS Compliance Overview
description: Security requirements for defense contractors under CMMC Level 2 and NIST 800-171.
content:
# CMMC & DFARS Compliance Overview

Defense contractors handling Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) must meet CMMC Level 2 requirements.

## What is CMMC?
Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification requiring contractors to implement 110 security practices from NIST SP 800-171.

## Security Awareness Requirements
NIST 800-171 Control 3.2.1 requires organizational personnel to be aware of security risks.

## Phishing Simulation Requirement
Control 3.2.2 requires training personnel to recognize and report potential indicators of insider threat.

## Consequences of Non-Compliance
Loss of DoD contracts and potential criminal charges under the False Claims Act.

title: NY DFS Part 500 Cybersecurity
description: New York cybersecurity regulation requirements for financial services companies.
content:
# NY DFS Part 500 Cybersecurity Regulation

NY DFS Part 500 applies to all financial services companies licensed in New York.

## Training Requirements
23 NYCRR 500.14(a) requires regular cybersecurity awareness training for all personnel.

## What the Training Must Cover
- Social engineering and phishing
- Secure use of company systems
- Password management
- Incident reporting procedures

## Annual Certification
The CISO must certify annually to the NY DFS Superintendent that the organization is in compliance.

## Penalties
NY DFS can impose fines of up to $1,000 per violation per day.

title: Zero Trust Security Principles
description: Understand the zero trust model and why perimeter-based security is obsolete.
content:
# Zero Trust Security Principles

Never trust, always verify is the core principle of zero trust.

## Why Zero Trust?
Traditional perimeter security assumed everything inside the network was safe. Remote work and cloud have made this assumption dangerous.

## Zero Trust Pillars
- Identity: Verify every user with MFA, every time
- Device: Only allow managed, compliant devices
- Network: Micro-segment networks, assume breach
- Application: Least-privilege access to each app

## What This Means for You
- You will be asked to authenticate more frequently
- Access is granted based on need, not location
